questions.q: Which statement best defines a project?
choices: An ongoing function that sustains organizational operations
A temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result
A collection of unrelated operational activities
A permanent organizational department
questions.explanation: A project is temporary and creates a unique product, service, or result. Temporary means it has a defined beginning and end.
whyWrong: Ongoing functions are operations, not projects.
A project is a coordinated temporary endeavor, not merely a collection of unrelated activities.
A permanent department is part of the organizational structure, not a project.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: Which characteristic most clearly distinguishes a project from operations?
choices: Projects are temporary, while operations are ongoing
Projects always require external vendors
Operations never use plans
Projects always last less than one year
questions.explanation: Projects are temporary. Operations are ongoing activities that sustain products, services, and organizational functions.
whyWrong: Vendor involvement is optional and does not define a project.
Operations may be carefully planned and controlled.
Temporary does not mean less than one year; a project may last several years.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: Which statement about project deliverables is correct?
choices: They must always be physical products
They may include products, services, or results
They stop providing benefits when the project ends
They are limited to financial outputs
questions.explanation: Projects can produce tangible or intangible deliverables, including products, services, or other results.
whyWrong: Projects can create intangible results, such as a process or capability.
Deliverables may continue providing benefits after the project ends.
Project outcomes are not limited to financial outputs.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: An organization performs the same customer billing activities every month. How should this work be classified?
choices: A project because it produces an output
Operations because it is ongoing and repetitive
A program because it contains multiple activities
A portfolio because it supports strategy
questions.explanation: Recurring billing is ongoing work that sustains the organization, so it is operations.
whyWrong: Producing an output does not automatically make work a project.
A program coordinates related projects, not routine monthly work.
Strategic relevance alone does not make routine work a portfolio.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: A company is developing and launching a new customer portal. How should this effort be classified?
choices: Operations
A project
A portfolio
A routine procedure
questions.explanation: Developing and launching a new portal is temporary and creates a unique result, so it is a project.
whyWrong: Ongoing support after launch may be operations, but the development effort is temporary.
A portfolio is a strategic grouping of work.
A unique development effort is not a routine procedure.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: A project is nearing completion, and an operations team will support the delivered system. What should the project manager do?
choices: Delay operations involvement until after closing
Coordinate transfer of deliverables, knowledge, and resources to operations
Keep project knowledge within the project team
Convert the project into a portfolio
questions.explanation: Projects commonly transfer deliverables, resources, and knowledge to operations so outcomes can be implemented and sustained.
whyWrong: Late involvement increases transition risk.
Knowledge must be transferred so the result can be sustained.
A transition to operations does not turn a project into a portfolio.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: Why should operations personnel be engaged before a project closes?
choices: They can help ensure the outcome can be implemented and sustained
They eliminate the need for governance
They automatically become the sponsor
They prevent every project risk
questions.explanation: Early operations involvement improves readiness, supportability, and long-term sustainability.
whyWrong: Operations involvement supports governance but does not eliminate it.
Operations personnel do not automatically become the sponsor.
No stakeholder group can prevent every risk.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.

questions.q: What does value mean in the PMBOK® Guide, Eighth Edition context?
choices: The number of activities completed
The excess of financial and nonfinancial benefits over the investment
The amount of budget spent
The length of the project schedule
questions.explanation: Value includes financial and nonfinancial benefits in excess of the investment used to achieve goals.
whyWrong: Activity completion is an output measure, not value itself.
Spending does not demonstrate benefit.
Schedule length does not establish value.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: Why may stakeholders perceive the value of the same project differently?
choices: They may have different needs, expectations, and desired benefits
Only the sponsor is allowed to define value
Value is always based only on revenue
Stakeholders do not influence project success
questions.explanation: Stakeholders may emphasize different quantitative or qualitative benefits.
whyWrong: Value is not defined only by the sponsor.
Value can be financial or nonfinancial.
Stakeholder perceptions are central to success and value.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: A project is on time and within budget, but intended users say the outcome does not meet their needs. What is the best conclusion?
choices: The project is automatically successful
The project may not be successful because expected value was not delivered
The project is successful because cost and schedule are the only measures
The work should now be classified as operations
questions.explanation: Project success is broader than cost and schedule; stakeholder needs, outcomes, and delivered value also matter.
whyWrong: Time and budget performance alone are insufficient.
Cost and schedule are important but not the only success measures.
Failure to meet needs does not automatically reclassify work as operations.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: A team must choose between a feature-rich solution and one that better supports strategic objectives and stakeholder needs. What should guide the decision?
choices: Choose the option with the most features
Choose the option expected to deliver the greatest overall value
Choose whichever option was proposed first
Choose the option requiring the most resources
questions.explanation: Decisions should be guided by strategic alignment, stakeholder needs, expected benefits, and overall value.
whyWrong: More features do not necessarily mean more value.
The order of proposal is irrelevant.
Using more resources does not establish better value.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: A change request will increase cost but significantly improve the expected benefits. What should the project manager consider first?
choices: Reject it because all cost increases are unacceptable
Evaluate its effect on the overall value proposition and project objectives
Approve it without analysis
Ask the team to implement it informally
questions.explanation: Changes should be evaluated against objectives, expected benefits, investment, and the overall value proposition.
whyWrong: A cost increase may be justified by greater value.
Changes require analysis and appropriate approval.
Informal implementation bypasses governance.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: Which outcome most clearly demonstrates value delivery?
choices: Completing every planned meeting
Reducing service wait time while maintaining quality
Using the entire approved budget
Producing the largest possible report
questions.explanation: A meaningful improvement in an outcome or benefit is a clearer measure of value than activity volume.
whyWrong: Meetings are activities, not necessarily benefits.
Budget consumption is not value.
A larger report is not automatically more useful.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: Which statement best describes project success?
choices: Every original activity was completed without change
Stakeholders and intended beneficiaries agree the project delivered value worth the effort and expense
The entire approved budget was spent
No risks occurred during the project
questions.explanation: Success reflects a consensus that the project delivered worthwhile value.
whyWrong: Projects may adapt and still succeed.
Spending the budget is not itself success.
All projects contain uncertainty; success does not require zero risk.
questions.clue: Do not rely only on scope, schedule, and cost; consider outcomes and stakeholder agreement.

questions.q: Key stakeholders disagree about what success means. What should the project manager do first?
choices: Wait until closing to discuss success
Facilitate alignment on objectives, expected outcomes, and success criteria
Use only the project manager's definition
Focus only on schedule performance
questions.explanation: Success criteria should be clarified and aligned early so decisions can be made against shared expectations.
whyWrong: Waiting creates misalignment and rework.
The project manager should facilitate, not impose a unilateral definition.
Schedule is only one aspect of success.
questions.clue: Do not rely only on scope, schedule, and cost; consider outcomes and stakeholder agreement.

questions.q: A project delivers the planned product, but the organization does not realize the expected benefits. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The project was fully successful because the deliverable was accepted
Benefits and value should be considered when assessing success
Benefits are never relevant to projects
The project automatically becomes a program
questions.explanation: Deliverable acceptance is important, but expected outcomes, benefits, and value are also relevant to success.
whyWrong: Acceptance alone may not demonstrate worthwhile value.
Benefits are relevant to the reason the project was undertaken.
A benefits shortfall does not change the work into a program.
questions.clue: Do not rely only on scope, schedule, and cost; consider outcomes and stakeholder agreement.

questions.q: Which components may all contribute to an organization's value delivery system?
choices: Only projects
Only programs and portfolios
Portfolios, programs, projects, products, and operations
Only operations
questions.explanation: These components can generate value individually and collectively.
whyWrong: Projects are only one component.
Programs and portfolios are not the only components.
Operations also contribute to value.
questions.clue: Think about how projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations work together.

questions.q: What is a value delivery system?
choices: A detailed project schedule
A collection of strategic business activities used to build, sustain, or advance an organization
A list of approved vendors
A financial accounting system
questions.explanation: A value delivery system aligns organizational work and components with strategy and desired outcomes.
whyWrong: A schedule is only one management artifact.
Vendor lists do not constitute the system.
Financial systems may support value delivery but are not the whole system.
questions.clue: Think about how projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations work together.

questions.q: What is a program?
choices: A group of related projects managed in a coordinated manner to obtain benefits not available from managing them separately
A single large project
A collection of unrelated operations
A list of project activities
questions.explanation: Programs coordinate related projects to obtain combined benefits and control.
whyWrong: Size alone does not make a project a program.
Programs are not simply unrelated operations.
A task list is not a program.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: What is a portfolio?
choices: A collection of programs, projects, and operations managed as a group to achieve strategic objectives
A single project's schedule and budget
A group containing only related projects
A department's routine procedures
questions.explanation: A portfolio aligns selected work with strategy and maximizes organizational value.
whyWrong: A schedule and budget belong to one project.
Portfolios may include related or unrelated work aligned by strategy.
Routine procedures are not a portfolio.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: Which sequence moves from the broadest grouping to the most specific?
choices: Project → Program → Portfolio
Portfolio → Program → Project
Program → Portfolio → Project
Operations → Portfolio → Project
questions.explanation: A portfolio may contain programs, and programs may contain related projects.
whyWrong: This reverses the hierarchy.
A portfolio is broader than a program.
Operations are not the top level of this hierarchy.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: Several related projects share resources and collectively deliver a major organizational capability. How should they most likely be managed?
choices: As unrelated operations
As a program
As a single task
As a permanent department
questions.explanation: Related projects managed together for combined benefits are a program.
whyWrong: The work is temporary and related, not routine operations.
A set of projects is broader than a single task.
A temporary coordinated effort is not a permanent department.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: An executive group is selecting and prioritizing projects and programs based on strategy and available investment. What level of management is this?
choices: Project management
Program management
Portfolio management
Operations management
questions.explanation: Portfolio management selects, prioritizes, and balances work to achieve strategic objectives and maximize value.
whyWrong: Project management focuses on one project.
Program management coordinates related projects.
Operations management sustains ongoing functions.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: Why should portfolio, program, project, and operations leaders coordinate?
choices: They may compete for resources and engage the same stakeholders
They must use identical development approaches
Operations always control every project
Projects cannot create value without becoming programs
questions.explanation: Coordination helps balance resources, stakeholder demands, dependencies, and strategic priorities.
whyWrong: Different work may require different approaches.
Authority depends on governance, not a universal rule.
Projects can create value independently.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: What does project governance provide?
choices: A framework, functions, and processes that guide decisions and activities toward value delivery
Only a project calendar
Only the project manager's job description
A replacement for stakeholder engagement
questions.explanation: Governance guides authority, oversight, alignment, decision-making, and value delivery.
whyWrong: A calendar is only a planning tool.
A job description does not define the full governance system.
Governance and stakeholder engagement complement each other.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: Should project governance be identical for every project?
choices: Yes, every project requires the same controls
No, governance should be tailored to the project and organizational context
Yes, but only for adaptive projects
No, because projects do not require governance
questions.explanation: Governance should be appropriate for the industry's requirements, organizational structure, project complexity, and development approach.
whyWrong: One-size-fits-all governance can create waste or gaps.
Adaptive projects still require tailored governance.
Projects require appropriate governance.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: A small adaptive project is subject to the same extensive controls used for a large regulated program. What should the project manager recommend?
choices: Keep every control because governance must be identical
Tailor governance while preserving required oversight and compliance
Remove all governance because the approach is adaptive
Transfer governance responsibility to the customer
questions.explanation: Governance should be tailored without removing mandatory oversight or compliance.
whyWrong: Excessive controls may waste resources.
Adaptive work still needs governance.
Governance responsibility is defined by the organization, not simply transferred.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: What can result from excessive governance?
choices: Unnecessary use of resources and slower decision-making
Automatic delivery of more value
Elimination of stakeholder needs
Conversion of the project into a portfolio
questions.explanation: Too much governance can create overhead, delay, and wasted effort.
whyWrong: More governance does not automatically mean more value.
Governance does not eliminate stakeholder needs.
Governance level does not determine whether work is a portfolio.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: What can result from insufficient governance?
choices: Guaranteed early completion
Strategic misalignment and project performance problems
Automatic cost savings
Elimination of uncertainty
questions.explanation: Too little governance can produce unclear authority, weak oversight, misalignment, and performance issues.
whyWrong: Insufficient governance does not guarantee speed or success.
Reduced oversight may increase costs.
Uncertainty cannot be eliminated.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: A decision exceeds the project manager's authority. What should the project manager do?
choices: Make the decision privately to avoid delay
Use the defined governance and escalation path
Ask any available team member to approve it
Ignore the issue
questions.explanation: Governance defines authority and escalation paths for decisions outside the project manager's delegated authority.
whyWrong: Acting outside delegated authority violates governance.
Approval must come from the authorized role.
Ignoring the issue creates risk and delay.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: A sponsor asks the project manager to implement a major scope change without documenting it. What is the best response?
choices: Implement it immediately because the sponsor requested it
Follow the project's governance and change-control approach
Ask the team to make the change unofficially
Wait until closing to record it
questions.explanation: Even sponsor-requested changes should follow the applicable governance and change process.
whyWrong: Sponsor authority does not eliminate required governance.
Unofficial changes undermine control and traceability.
Late documentation creates risk and poor decision records.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: Who may perform project management responsibilities?
choices: Only someone formally titled Project Manager
Different roles depending on the governance structure and project context
Only the sponsor
Only a certified portfolio manager
questions.explanation: Project management functions may be performed by project managers, functional managers, product owners, team leads, or others depending on context.
whyWrong: The function matters more than the title.
Sponsors provide support and direction but do not necessarily manage daily work.
Portfolio management is a different level of work.
questions.clue: The function and responsibility matter more than the formal job title.

questions.q: What matters more than the formal title of the person overseeing a project?
choices: The size of the person's department
The characteristics of the project and proper fulfillment of project management functions
The person's office location
The number of direct reports
questions.explanation: The nature of the project and the functions performed matter more than a specific job title.
whyWrong: Department size does not determine project management effectiveness.
Office location is irrelevant.
Direct-report count does not define the role.
questions.clue: The function and responsibility matter more than the formal job title.

questions.q: Who is a stakeholder?
choices: Only the sponsor and project manager
Anyone who can affect, be affected by, or perceive themselves to be affected by the project
Only people receiving project funds
Only members of the project team
questions.explanation: Stakeholders include individuals, groups, and organizations that affect or are affected by the project.
whyWrong: Stakeholders extend beyond these two roles.
Financial recipients are only one possible stakeholder group.
Team members are stakeholders, but they are not the only stakeholders.
questions.clue: The function and responsibility matter more than the formal job title.

questions.q: A project manager discovers that an important user group was not consulted during planning. What should be done first?
choices: Continue because planning is already complete
Identify and engage the overlooked stakeholders to understand their needs and impact
Remove the user group from the stakeholder list
Wait until testing begins
questions.explanation: Stakeholders should be identified and engaged early enough to influence objectives, requirements, and value.
whyWrong: Continuing without engagement increases risk.
Excluding affected users is inappropriate.
Waiting may cause avoidable rework.
questions.clue: The function and responsibility matter more than the formal job title.

questions.q: Project conditions change and the original approach no longer supports the expected outcome. What should the project manager do?
choices: Follow the original plan regardless of evidence
Adapt the approach while preserving alignment, governance, and value
Cancel every deliverable
Avoid informing stakeholders
questions.explanation: Effective project management adapts to context while maintaining strategic alignment and value.
whyWrong: Rigid adherence may reduce value.
Cancellation is not automatically required.
Stakeholder communication is essential during change.
questions.clue: Prefer responsible tailoring that preserves compliance, alignment, and value.

questions.q: A team proposes a simpler process that meets governance requirements and reduces delay. What is the best action?
choices: Reject it because the original process cannot change
Evaluate and adopt it if it improves value and remains compliant
Adopt it without review
Use both processes permanently
questions.explanation: Tailoring and continuous improvement are appropriate when they preserve required controls and improve outcomes.
whyWrong: Processes may be tailored when justified.
Review is required before adoption.
Duplicating processes may add waste.
questions.clue: Prefer responsible tailoring that preserves compliance, alignment, and value.

questions.q: A schedule report is produced every week, but no stakeholder uses it to make decisions. What should the project manager consider?
choices: Continue because every project must produce the same reports
Tailor reporting to provide useful information that supports value and decisions
Increase the report length
Stop all communication
questions.explanation: Artifacts and reports should serve a useful purpose; tailoring can remove waste while preserving needed transparency.
whyWrong: Reporting should be appropriate to context.
Length does not create usefulness.
Communication should be improved, not eliminated.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: Which action most improves the likelihood that project success will be assessed consistently?
choices: Define success only after delivery
Agree on measurable and qualitative success criteria with stakeholders early
Use only budget variance
Let each stakeholder use an undisclosed definition
questions.explanation: Early agreement on success criteria helps align expectations and supports consistent assessment.
whyWrong: Late definition invites disagreement.
Budget variance is only one measure.
Undisclosed definitions create confusion.
questions.clue: Do not rely only on scope, schedule, and cost; consider outcomes and stakeholder agreement.

questions.q: A project no longer supports organizational strategy, even though it is performing well. What should portfolio leadership consider?
choices: Continue it solely because it is on schedule
Reassess its priority and continued investment based on strategic value
Convert it into operations automatically
Increase its budget
questions.explanation: Portfolio decisions should reflect strategic alignment and value, not only project execution performance.
whyWrong: Good execution does not justify strategically misaligned work.
Reclassification does not solve strategic misalignment.
More budget may worsen value.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: A project team is unclear about who can approve funding changes. What is the most appropriate action?
choices: Let the team vote
Clarify decision authority in the governance framework
Allow the vendor to decide
Avoid funding changes entirely
questions.explanation: Governance should make authority, accountability, and escalation paths clear.
whyWrong: Voting does not replace delegated authority.
Vendors do not typically hold organizational funding authority.
Avoidance does not resolve the governance gap.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: A project delivers a new capability, but no operational process exists to use it. What is the primary concern?
choices: The project has too many stakeholders
The value delivery system is incomplete because the capability may not be sustained or used
The project should become a portfolio
The schedule is automatically invalid
questions.explanation: Value often depends on integration between projects and operations so delivered capabilities can produce ongoing benefits.
whyWrong: Stakeholder count is not the primary issue.
A missing operational process does not make the work a portfolio.
The schedule may still be valid even though value realization is at risk.
questions.clue: Think about how projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations work together.

questions.q: A sponsor asks the project manager to accelerate delivery by removing a validation activity. The activity is the main way users confirm that the outcome meets their needs. What should the project manager do first?
choices: Remove the activity because the sponsor requested it
Assess the effect on expected value, quality, and stakeholder acceptance before deciding
Ask the team to work overtime and keep the activity
Wait until closing to determine whether validation was necessary
questions.explanation: The first step is to assess the impact on value and stakeholder acceptance before making or escalating the decision.
whyWrong: Sponsor requests still require informed analysis and governance.
Overtime may not address the underlying trade-off and should not be the first response.
Waiting until closing would create avoidable risk and rework.
questions.clue: Focus on benefits, stakeholder needs, and whether the outcome is worth the investment.

questions.q: Two leaders give the project team conflicting directions about a major decision. What should the project manager do next?
choices: Follow the direction from the most senior person without checking authority
Use the governance framework to clarify decision authority and resolve the conflict
Let the team choose which direction it prefers
Pause all project work indefinitely
questions.explanation: Governance should identify who has decision authority and how conflicts are escalated.
whyWrong: Seniority alone may not equal delegated project authority.
Team preference does not replace governance.
A targeted escalation is more appropriate than stopping all work indefinitely.
questions.clue: Look for authority, accountability, decision rights, escalation, and appropriate tailoring.

questions.q: Near the end of a project, the sponsor says the deliverable is complete, but customers disagree that the expected outcome has been achieved. What should the project manager do first?
choices: Close the project because the sponsor is satisfied
Revisit the agreed success criteria and facilitate stakeholder alignment on the evidence
Ask customers to accept the deliverable without discussion
Measure only final cost variance
questions.explanation: The project manager should use previously agreed success criteria and evidence to align stakeholders before concluding whether the project succeeded.
whyWrong: Sponsor satisfaction alone may not establish success.
Forced acceptance does not resolve the disagreement.
Cost variance is only one measure.
questions.clue: Do not rely only on scope, schedule, and cost; consider outcomes and stakeholder agreement.

questions.q: A project is performing well but no longer supports the organization's strategic priorities. Who should primarily reassess whether investment should continue?
choices: The individual project team alone
Portfolio leadership using strategic value and prioritization criteria
The vendor
Operations staff only
questions.explanation: Portfolio leadership evaluates whether investments remain aligned with strategy and continue to justify resources.
whyWrong: The project team can provide information but does not normally own portfolio prioritization.
A vendor does not own organizational investment decisions.
Operations input may be useful but is not the sole decision authority.
questions.clue: Programs coordinate related projects; portfolios align investments with strategy.

questions.q: A project uses a product owner instead of someone formally titled project manager. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The work cannot be considered a project
Project management responsibilities may be performed by different roles depending on context
Only the sponsor may coordinate the work
Governance is unnecessary when a product owner is present
questions.explanation: The PMBOK framework emphasizes the project management functions being performed rather than requiring one universal title.
whyWrong: A specific title is not required for work to be a project.
Sponsors do not necessarily perform daily coordination.
All projects need appropriate governance.
questions.clue: The function and responsibility matter more than the formal job title.

questions.q: A required report takes several hours to prepare, but stakeholders use only one section. What is the best action?
choices: Stop reporting without notifying anyone
Tailor the report with stakeholder and governance approval so it remains useful and compliant
Continue the full report forever because it existed in the original plan
Add more sections to increase its value
questions.explanation: Tailoring should remove waste while preserving transparency, compliance, and stakeholder needs.
whyWrong: Unilateral removal may violate governance or stakeholder expectations.
Plans may be adapted when justified.
More content does not necessarily create more value.
questions.clue: Prefer responsible tailoring that preserves compliance, alignment, and value.

questions.q: A project successfully delivers a new service, but the organization has not funded the operations needed to sustain it. What is the greatest concern?
choices: The project used too few meetings
The expected value may not be realized because the service cannot be sustained
The work must be converted into a program
The project schedule is automatically invalid
questions.explanation: Value realization depends on the broader value delivery system, including operations that sustain the delivered service.
whyWrong: Meeting volume is not the core issue.
A missing operating model does not automatically make the work a program.
The schedule may still be valid even though benefits are at risk.
questions.clue: Think about how projects, programs, portfolios, products, and operations work together.

questions.q: After a project launches a new system, a permanent team handles routine support and recurring updates. How should that support work be classified?
choices: A continuation of the original project forever
Operations
A portfolio
A project charter
questions.explanation: Routine, ongoing support is operations, even though the capability was originally created by a project.
whyWrong: A project is temporary and does not continue forever.
A portfolio is a strategic grouping of work.
A charter is an authorization document, not ongoing work.
questions.clue: Look for whether the work is temporary and unique or ongoing and sustaining.
